task_context.description: This is a comparative detector design and performance evaluation task focused on precision timing measurements for calorimetry applications. The task involves designing and simulating three different calorimeter detector concepts to achieve ultra-fast timing resolution in the 10-30 picosecond range. It requires systematic comparison of detector technologies including fast crystal scintillators, plastic scintillator tiles, and sampling calorimeters with different material combinations. The evaluation must assess both timing performance and energy resolution across multiple energy points to determine optimal detector architecture for future collider applications where pileup rejection is critical.
task_context.goal: Determine which detector concept provides the best timing performance while maintaining reasonable calorimetric energy resolution for pileup rejection at future colliders
constraints: Timing resolution target of approximately 10-30 picoseconds
Must test exactly 3 configurations (one per concept)
Energy sweep limited to 1, 5, 20 GeV for tractable simulation
Must use electrons (e-) in Geant4 particle gun
LYSO crystal must use homogeneous_slab or box topology
Plastic scintillator must use segmented topology with tiles
Sampling calorimeter must use box or shashlik topology with tungsten absorber

observations.content: This workflow systematically compares three detector concepts (LYSO crystal, plastic scintillator tiles, tungsten-plastic sampling) for ultra-fast timing calorimetry. The approach generates separate geometries for each concept, then runs a comprehensive simulation across all configurations and energy points. The analysis focuses on extracting timing resolution from first-hit distributions and energy resolution from total energy deposits. A key innovation is the combined simulation step that tests all detector concepts efficiently within the computational budget. The workflow balances timing performance against energy resolution to identify the optimal detector architecture for pileup rejection at future colliders, with the target of 10-30 picosecond timing resolution.
Step completed successfully: Generate GDML geometry for LYSO crystal homogeneous calorimeter with box topology for fast timing measurements

Step skipped: Failure signature recurring (3x) — step is stuck in a loop. Last error: geometry: Custom geometry validation failed:

Step completed successfully: Generate GDML geometry for tungsten-plastic sampling calorimeter with shashlik topology for timing measurements

methodology_notes: Planned 10-step workflow: generate_lyso_crystal_geometry, generate_plastic_segmented_geometry, generate_sampling_calorimeter_geometry, simulate_all_detector_concepts, analyze_timing_performance, analyze_energy_resolution, compare_detector_performance, generate_performance_plots, evaluate_design_goals, generate_final_report
